author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
God Enjoins No Impossibility, Because All Things are Possible and Easy to Love.
title: God Enjoins No Impossibility, Because All Things are Possible and Easy to Love.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Chapter 83 [LXIX.]—God Enjoins No Impossibility, Because All Things are Possible and Easy to Love.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: But “the precepts of the law are very good,” if we use them lawfully.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Indeed, by the very fact (of which we have the firmest conviction) “that the just and good God could not possibly have enjoined impossibilities,” we are admonished both what to do in easy paths and what to ask for when they are difficult.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Now all things are easy for love to effect, to which (and which alone) “Christ’s burden is light,”—or rather, it is itself alone the burden which is light.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Accordingly it is said, “And His commandments are not grievous;” so that whoever finds them grievous must regard the inspired statement about their “not being grievous” as having been capable of only this meaning, that there may be a state of heart to which they are not burdensome, and he must pray for that disposition which he at present wants, so as to be able to fulfil all that is commanded him.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: And this is the purport of what is said to Israel in Deuteronomy, if understood in a godly, sacred, and spiritual sense, since the apostle, after quoting the passage, “The word is nigh thee, even in thy mouth and in thy heart” (and, as the verse also has it, in thine hands, for in man’s heart are his spiritual hands), adds in explanation, “This is the word of faith which we preach.”

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: No man, therefore, who “returns to the Lord his God,” as he is there commanded, “with all his heart and with all his soul,” will find God’s commandment “grievous.”

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: How, indeed, can it be grievous, when it is the precept of love?

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Either, therefore, a man has not love, and then it is grievous; or he has love, and then it is not grievous.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: But he possesses love if he does what is there enjoined on Israel, by returning to the Lord his God with all his heart and with all his soul.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: “A new commandment,” says He, “do I give unto you, that ye love one another;” and “He that loveth his neighbour hath fulfilled the law;” and again, “Love is the fulfilling of the law.”

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: In accordance with these sayings is that passage, “Had they trodden good paths, they would have found, indeed, the ways of righteousness easy.”

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: How then is it written, “Because of the words of Thy lips, I have kept the paths of difficulty,” except it be that both statements are true: These paths are paths of difficulty to fear; but to love they are easy?
